Climbing Mount Cameroon was never supposed to happen that day.

We arrived in Cameroon early just to recover a checkpoint we had missed before. The plan sounded simple enough: reach the highest point in the country and continue the journey. Instead, it started with a delayed flight, missing luggage, and barely two hours of sleep before standing at the trailhead.

Most hikers take two days to reach the summit. The distance is not extreme, but the mountain rises almost straight out of the rainforest with nearly 3,000 meters of elevation gain. With a guide and a rally mindset, we decided to try it in a single push.

The trail does not ease you in. There are no gentle switchbacks, only a steady climb that sometimes turns into scrambling on all fours. After losing our original guide early on, we unexpectedly found another one higher up the mountain, continuing together toward the summit through mist and volcanic terrain.

Seven and a half hours later we reached the top of Mount Cameroon.

The descent turned out to be the harder part. Rain made the rocks slippery, progress slowed, and the final stretch through the jungle happened after dark with unfamiliar animal sounds echoing through the forest. It took another six careful hours to reach the bottom.

Mount Cameroon is not just the highest peak in the country. It is a long, direct, and surprisingly demanding climb that feels much bigger than the map suggests — especially when done in a single day with almost no sleep beforehand.
